name: Golden Bantam
image_alt: Golden yellow sweet corn ears on tall stalks
description: The heirloom sweet corn that defined what corn should taste like before hybrid varieties changed the standard. Small 6-inch ears with golden yellow kernels and a rich, full corn flavor with balanced sweetness.
history: Introduced in 1902 by W. Atlee Burpee, Golden Bantam was the first widely available yellow sweet corn at a time when white corn dominated. It revolutionized home corn growing and remained the standard until hybrid varieties displaced it in the 1950s.

good.reason: Ground cover, reduces weeds and moisture loss

seasonal_quirks: Needs warm soil (60°F+) to germinate well. Block planting is essential — corn is wind-pollinated and single rows produce poorly. Peak flavor is within hours of harvest as sugars convert to starch rapidly.
harvest_cues: Silks should be brown and dry. Pierce a kernel — milky juice means peak ripeness. Watery juice means too early, pasty means too late. Usually 3 weeks after silks appear.
variety_notes: Flavor surpasses modern supersweet hybrids in complexity — less cloying sweetness, more genuine corn character. Must be eaten immediately after harvest for best results.
preservation: Blanch and freeze kernels. Dry and grind into cornmeal.
